explanation_used:
1. **Opening statement**: This neuron strongly responds to **abstract concepts of inner essence, emotion, or intangible vitality**, particularly when expressed through the keywords “spirit” or “soul” (such as *“Teen Spirit,” “community spirit,” “Spirit of God,” “touches the soul,” “soulful rendition,”* or *“soul mate”*).

2. **Domain coverage**: The pattern appears predominantly in English texts discussing music, awards, community events, religion, and emotional or metaphysical experiences. Examples span music genres (R&B, soul, pop), cultural awards (Soul Train Awards, Young Spirit Award), religious references (Spirit of God), and expressions of emotional depth (“troubled soul,” “lifts spirits”). The neuron also moderately activates for the phrase “in order to” in goal-oriented contexts, but the strongest responses are tied explicitly to “spirit/soul” lexicon.

3. **Contrast with low-activation examples**: High-activation texts consistently contain the terms “spirit” or “soul” (or their derivatives) in abstract, non-literal contexts, whereas low-activation examples are mostly news fragments, technical announcements, or non-English phrases that lack any reference to these abstract emotional/spiritual concepts. The low-activation group focuses on concrete events, factual reporting, or procedural language without metaphorical or affective depth.

4. **Closing interpretation**: The neuron detects linguistic markers of intangible human qualities—emotional states, cultural vitality, or metaphysical essence—primarily through the lexical anchors “spirit” and “soul.” It functions as a semantic filter for expressions of inner life or collective ethos.

results.actual_text: Although the shooter Brenton Tarrant was Australian, there has been a lot of soul searching in NZ about whether they had allowed views like Tarrant’s to flourish in
results.plain_text: Did tolerance cause terror in Christchurch?  With the emergency response over experts are now trying to work out how last Friday's terror attack in Christchurch which killed 50 people could have happened. Although the shooter Brenton Tarrant was Australian, there has been a lot of soul searching in NZ about whether they had allowed views like Tarrant’s to flourish in the name of freedom of speech. New Zealand is renowned as a tolerant nation and this may well have made the country Tarrant’s target. Some experts believe the New Zealand Government has not focussed enough on the potential for terror attacks by these groups and instead has focussed on the possibility of a Muslim terrorist attack. Guests  Paul Spoonley Paul Spoonley, distinguished professor at Massey University in NZ and pro-vice chancellor or its College of Humanities and Social Sciences Credits  Presenter Phillip Adams Producer Tim Brunero

predictions.reasoning: Contains the keyword 'soul' in an abstract introspective context about national reflection.

results.plain_text: PASIR GUDANG, March 19 — The section of Sungai Kim Kim where toxic waste was illegally dumped has been cleaned up and is now safe, Energy, Science, Technology, Environment and Climate Change Minister Yeo Bee Yin said today.  She said the authorities will, however, continue to monitor the situation at the site for the next 15 days.  “In fact, the entire 1.5km stretch of Sungai Kim Kim that was contaminated before this has been declared safe.  “The cleaning work by the contractors that started on March 13 and ended on March 17 saw a total of 900 tonnes of contaminated substance and 1,500 metric tonnes of contaminated water cleared,” Yeo told a press conference at Sungai Kim Kim’s main pollution site in Taman Pasir Puteh here.  Yeo said today was the first time that the affected area in Sungai Kim Kim has been declared safe.  “The air quality around here has shown zero parts per million (of chemical pollutants) and we are constantly monitoring the air quality.  “In order to convince people, especially locals, that the area is clear of pollution, I have visited the area without wearing safety equipment,” said Yeo.  Yeo, who is also the Bakri MP, explained that the pollutants are being systematically removed and stored away, and they will all be sent for scheduled disposal by March 21.  She said a complete report on the clean-up operation, new pollution discoveries at two other locations and other issues pertaining to the toxic waste incident will be announced at a press conference tomorrow.  It was reported that more than 4,000 people have been affected and 111 schools closed following the chemical waste incident at the river on March 7.

predictions.text: “The air quality around here has shown zero parts per million (of chemical pollutants) and we are constantly monitoring the air quality.  “In order to convince people, especially locals, that the area is clear of pollution, I have visited the area without wearing safety equipment,” said Yeo.
predictions.reasoning: Contains the phrase 'in order to' in a goal-oriented context, which the neuron moderately activates for.

It was reported that more than 4,000 people have been affected and 111 schools closed following the chemical waste incident at the river on March 7.

However, other nationalities and regions around the world have taken notice. During the Korean War, U.S. troops introduced fried chicken to the local population, as military members celebrated Thanksgiving with fried chicken, instead of turkey, which was not easily available to them. While the military eventually left, the idea of fried chicken remained. Since then, South Korea has crafted its own fried chicken recipes and flavors, unique to them with their available spices and flavors. It has grown increasingly popular over the decades, and now a Korean-based fried chicken restaurant is making its way to metro Phoenix. macaronikid.com Since we are getting ready to go into Black History Month, I thought it was a good idea to talk about our cuisine! Southern cuisine is a unique blend of traditional cooking styles, flavors, and ingredients that have been passed down over generations. The cuisine has its roots in the agricultural and agrarian lifestyles of the Southern United States and was heavily influenced by the foods brought over from Africa. Some common ingredients used were okra, bush greens (collard greens for example), yams (which are different from sweet potatoes), eggplant, black-eyed peas, sesame seeds, cornmeal and red rice. Meat was a rarity, so it was first only used for flavoring. Biscuits, cornbread, and corn pone are also popular dishes. Traditional Southern cooking is often served family-style, with large portions of food served on a single platter. Southern cuisine is also known for its use of spices, such as garlic, cayenne, and black pepper. With its deep roots in the past, many people still enjoy Southern cuisine. results.plain_text: BRATISLAVA, SLOVAKIA (BLOOMBERG) - The European nation that protected its population better than any other from Covid-19 at the start of the global pandemic is now trying to do one better - by testing its entire population for the virus.  Slovakia, a country of 5.5 million people on the European Union's eastern border, is preparing to launch a two-stage process using cheap antibody tests to help stop the resurgence in new cases that's sweeping across the continent.  Others have shown success in mass testing, with China canvassing cities with populations far exceeding Slovakia's and Taiwan not registering a domestic case in more than 200 days. But none has yet tried to test all of its citizens.  Encouraged by turnout of more than 90 per cent at a pilot round last weekend, Prime Minister Igor Matovic has vowed to make Slovakia the world's first country to know the full scope of contagion in its borders.  He has pushed the idea as an alternative to reimposing measures far harsher than in the spring, when lockdowns wiped out more than 10 per cent of most European economies.  "We will save hundreds of lives," he said this week.  While most testing uses so-called PCR kits aimed at identifying the virus in patients, Slovakia will rely on cheaper antibody tests in an experiment closely watched by the 27-member European Union.  This week, the bloc's executive commission pledged to spend €100 million (S$159 million) for member states to purchase the tests, which are able to provide results within 15 minutes.  The two-day programme will start on Saturday (Oct 31) at 7am and be repeated a week later.  Anyone who doesn't take part must show proof of a negative test to be able to move around in public.  In the weekend pilot, about 5,600 people, or 4 per cent, were positive and were put into quarantine.  The true rate may be as much as three times higher, however, as the test fails to identify cases in people who haven't yet developed an immune response.  Medical experts have expressed scepticism, as the authorities won't follow up positive cases with PCR tests and contact tracing, while the initiative may exhaust an already stretched healthcare system.  A day before starting, officials were still seeking enough volunteer medical personnel to administer the tests.  Critics suggest it's aimed more at shoring up Mr Matovic's flagging popularity among voters than eradicating the virus.  President Zuzana Caputova found out about the plan when Mr Matovic announced it during a news conference.  By that time, his government had already purchased 13 million testing kits for about US$5 (S$7) each.  "This isn't an expert operation, but a political operation," said Dr Marian Kollar, the head of Slovakia's Medical Chamber.  The country "shouldn't take steps that don't have any significant impact on the spread of the pandemic".

predictions.text: He has pushed the idea as an alternative to reimposing measures far harsher than in the spring, when lockdowns wiped out more than 10 per cent of most European economies. “We will save hundreds of lives,” he said this week.
predictions.reasoning: Emotional statement about preserving life, which may relate to intangible human value.

results.actual_text: The Friday night lights of Texas high school football have taken on a mythical quality over the
results.plain_text: The Friday night lights of Texas high school football have taken on a mythical quality over the years, but Thursdays have a storied history of their own. Author Michael Hurd will take part in a live virtual event for the Allen Public Library to discuss the largely unknown history of Black high school football in the state. Hurd’s Thursday Night Lights: The Story of Black High School Football in Texas was named one of the 30 best Texas books for the 2010-19 decade by The Texas Observer and one of the 30 most essential books about Texas by the Houston Chronicle. The live event will be at 7:30 p.m. May 13 at allentx.swagit.com. Former Dallas Morning News columnist James Ragland will moderate the program. Former Dallas Cowboys receiver Tony Hill will also take part. Thursday Night Lights tells the inspiring story of Black high school football in Texas from 1920 to 1970. The segregated high schools in the Prairie View Interscholastic League (the Black counterpart to the University Interscholastic League) included future NFL greats such as “Mean” Joe Greene, Otis Taylor and Charles “Bubba” Smith, who appeared in six Police Academy movies after his All-Pro career. Hurd is also the author of Black College Football, 1892-1992, the only book that comprehensively documents the legacies of football programs at historically Black colleges, and he serves on the selection committee for the Black College Football Hall of Fame. He is a board member for the Writers’ League of Texas, a trustee with the Friends of the Texas Historical Commission and a member of the Texas Institute of Letters. For details about the virtual event, call 214-509-4911.

predictions.text: Thursday Night Lights tells the inspiring story of Black high school football in Texas from 1920 to 1970. The segregated high schools in the Prairie View Interscholastic League (the Black counterpart to the University Interscholastic League) included future NFL greats such as “Mean” Joe Greene, Otis Taylor and Charles “Bubba” Smith, who appeared in six Police Academy movies after his All-Pro career.
predictions.reasoning: Contains the word 'inspiring' and describes an emotional narrative about historical achievement.
